data.title: GPIX Is Best For Dividend, But There Will Be Better Times To Buy
data.description:
Goldman Sachs S&P 500 Core Premium Income ETF (GPIX) is actually a traditional S&P 500 ETF, but it combines a traditional ETF strategy with a dynamic covered call to provide regular income. In this article, I will explain why I decided to "hold" GPIX instead of buy.

Fund Structure and Strategic Framework

ETF uses a dynamic option method which targets to hold at least 80% of its net assets in equity securities of companies that are part of the S&P 500 Index. It differs from both conventional buy-and-hold S&P 500 strategies and static covered call methods due to its dual strategy.

In addition to that, unlike systematic covered call strategies that maintain fixed margin ratios, GPIX dynamically adjusts option exposure between 25% and 75% of the portfolio value. Currently, 36% of the portfolio is options and that leaves significant upside potential.

Heston Model

I first analyze the ETF using the Heston model. According to the results of the analysis, GPIX's volatility since its launch is at 15.6%. The initial variance value is 0.0245 and the mean reversion speed is 2.00. When I look at the evolution of its volatility, I see that the median volatility will negatively depart from the long-term volatility of 15.6% and fall below 15% in the next 1 year according to the forecast. In addition to that, price path simulations show that the potential for the price to rise by 5.2% from its current level in the next 1 year is 28.5%. Plus, there is still a 26.1% downside risk.

Comparative Analysis and Competitive Positioning

First of all, I want to compare GPIX with its main substitute, SP500. In this way, we can get to know the fund much better. While making this comparison I first want to take a section from GPIX's market launch to the end of February. This section represents a bull market.

In a strong bull market, I see the SP500 rising by 44.62% from October 2023 to March 2025. The rise in GPIX in the same time period is only 26.87%. Due to its structure, it is quite normal and expected that GPIX's price performance is less than SP500. First of all, being an option-based ETF, its 0.29% expense rate and regular dividend payment limit GPIX's capital return. But dividend payments reduce the difference between SP500 and GPIX.

For example, when GPIX was offered to the public, the capital of an investor who invested $100 increased to $126.87, while the total dividend income earned was $13.72. This shows that the investor earned a total income of $140. During the same period, the capital of an investor who invested in SP500 increased from $100 to $144. So when dividends are added to the capital increase, the difference narrows a little more. The remaining 4.03% difference can be seen as the opportunity cost of earning regular income.

So GPIX seems to be very advantageous for investors who want to earn regular income, especially in bull markets. But is this the case in bear markets? Because I know that some ETFs perform much worse than bulls in bear markets.

If I need to explain my hesitation better for investors who are new to option-based ETFs, GPIX has partial downside protection. The ETF's collected premiums only compensate for losses equivalent to the dollar value. In serious declines, the 36% option coverage that I mentioned in the first paragraphs provides limited protection while the 64% that is not covered remains completely exposed.

In addition to that, while covered calls benefit from high volatility (higher premiums), sharp downside volatility can upset the defensive capabilities of the strategy, especially if puts are not used. Plus, in stressful situations, the fund's FLEX options can face wider bid-ask spreads which can reduce the effectiveness of the hedge.

So, does GPIX manage these risks well? I think it does very well. As is known, the Trump tariff news caused the market to drop significantly starting on February 19 and in a sense, put it in a partial bear market. The SP500 index lost approximately 21.13% of its value from February 19 to April 7. In the same time period, the GPIX lost 20.92%. So even though the return is slightly lower in the bull market, I think GPIX manages bear markets more effectively than SP500.

I want to take a look at the one-to-one substitutes for GPIX too. I think JEPI and SPYI are substitutes for GPIX. First, I want to talk about JEPI. Even though JEPI has a similar dividend yield to GPIX, it has much lower volatility than GPIX, but its price performance is just as low. This causes it not to provide enough RoC.

SPYI has a higher dividend yield than GPIX, but although its low volatility makes SPYI seem like a safer fund than GPIX its price performance is quite low. Another disadvantage is that its expense ratio is significantly higher than GPIX (0.68%) and its strategy is not dynamic enough compared to FLEX options.

So if JEPI is more advantageous for those who want a safer, higher dividend yield than GPIX and do not expect a return on capital, GPIX is much more logical in terms of return on capital, although it is more volatile.

Charting and Dividend Analysis

The dividend per share paid by GPIX is directly proportional to the price performance of GPIX. When I look at the dividend yield historically, I see that it fluctuates around the average of 0.71%. The dividend yield historically increased to a maximum of 0.74% and decreased to a minimum of 0.68%. Right now it continues to fluctuate within the channel. This shows me that there is an obvious positive correlation between the ETF price and dividend per share.

When linear regression analysis is applied to the price of the SP500 index since 2010, I can clearly see that the index is in an uptrend. This gives me the signal that if these conditions continue, the dividend per share paid by GPIX will increase from year to year.

I want to go a little shorter. The $48.35 level was a really important support and resistance level for GPIX since 2024. This area which was tested as resistance twice until September 2024, was tested as support 3 times after it was broken. After the tariff news along with the decline of the entire market, GPIX, which came back to this support level broken in March 2025, was testing this level for the 3rd time since last May. So I can say that the fund is currently in a really critical price action area.

Breaking this level could allow GPIX to start a new run and a peak of $50.80 could be seen again. In this scenario, with a dividend yield of 0.70% dividend per share would also increase to $0.36. If this level is not broken and GPIX is rejected from this strong resistance, it may pull back to $47.00 and continue to move in the side channel between $47-48.35. In this scenario, my calculation is that the dividend per share will be at least $0.33 with an average dividend yield of 0.70%.

So which scenario is more likely? I see megacap stocks such as MSFT, NVDA, GOOGL which make up a large portion of the stocks held by the index and GPIX, gaining momentum again. In addition to that, I see Trump's tariff stance softening day by day in line with the "TACO" (Trump Always Chickens Out) market thesis. Considering the very successful negotiations between the US and China on June 2, I think the bullish scenario is more prevalent at the moment.

Even though I say that the momentum of the megacap stocks that make up the index is high, I know that the serious runs they made will need to be corrected at some point, especially after returning from the lows they decreased in April. Considering that 7 of the 9 megacap stocks covering 35.92% of the GPIX currently have above-average RSIs and even 4 stocks covering 18.88% of the GPIX are in the overbought region, I can say that there is a potential for a correction in these stocks in the near future.

Stock RSI
NVDA 69.36
MSFT 71.58
AAPL 48.44
AMZN 59.84
META 72.36
AVGO 82.7
GOOGL 54.1
TSLA 53.75
BRK.B 34.87

This correction potential also reveals the possibility that the GPIX will be suppressed in the near future, which means that the scenario of moving in the side channel is also quite possible.

I think that there is no negative outlook ahead of us, so there is no reason to stay on the sell side. However, I think saying that GPIX will break the $48.35 resistance and run with solid momentum is a bit optimistic in a situation where most megacap stocks are in overbought territory. That's why I find "hold" much more logical for now.

Risks

GPIX is known as a FLEX option. Even though being a FLEX option provides the fund with the opportunity to customize since it has much lower liquidity compared to standard options, when volatility increases, bid-ask spreads can widen more than normal and reduce the effectiveness of the hedge.

In addition to that, since GPIX is a full SP500 exposure fund, it is fully correlated to general stock movements. This puts pressure on the index with uncertainty as long as President Trump does not clarify the tariff issue, and it also affects GPIX as a negative pressure too.

Conclusion

In conclusion, GPIX is a really good option for investors who want to invest in the SP500 index but focus not only on capital growth but also on regular income. So I don't have much doubt that the fund will rise in the long term. But since last month, the fund has been rejected from the $48.35 resistance level and the fact that the fund's stocks are in overbought areas increases the possibility that the fund may be under pressure in the near future. So even though I think the fund will tend to rise in the long term, I do not think it is in the buy area at the moment. That's why my stance is "hold" for now.

data.title: Venture Global: Rapidly Closing The Valuation Gap With Cheniere
data.description:
Venture Global operates and develops LNG export facilities along the Gulf Coast in Louisiana. The company uses modular liquefaction trains to reduce capital costs. The company is expanding its capacity at breakneck speed. June 2 FERC gave the green light to go ahead with its CP2 project. To quote:

If completed, CP2 would be the single largest LNG export facility in the U.S. and could make Venture Global (NYSE:VG) the largest U.S. LNG company with 66.5M tons/year, surpassing Cheniere Energy's (LNG) expected 60M tons/year of capacity by that time.

Just one day later, Venture Global PR’ed that the work is ongoing (emphasis mine):

Venture Global said CP2 is well advanced in engineering, procurement and contracting; separate from the site work that has started, the project already has significant off-site work underway on modules and equipment.

These releases have had a profound effect on the company's share price, with the stock up 28% since June 2:

I’ve highlighted VG as an interesting company in PDX: High Income And Opportunistic Bets At A Steep Discount. The article is about a closed-end fund that allocated an estimated ~20% of its capital into VG. In that article, I also discuss litigation that the company is dealing with. In the long-run, if the operations operate as planned, I don’t think it will matter much. The valuation will mostly depend on the margin the company is getting on its 20-year contracts and the soundness of capital allocation once the cash starts rolling in.

The company is very similar to Cheniere Energy, which is why I compared it to that company in my prior article. The idea behind the small modular units is that construction is cheaper, compared to Cheniere, and faster. The latter seems borne out by the speed at which the company has been moving in the last few days.

In terms of market cap, VG is still quite a lot behind LNG, meaning there’s upside potential. In terms of EV they’re somewhat closer, though:

VG is in full expansion mode, while LNG is only slowly expanding. In effect, LNG is far more mature of an operation. However, I’d expect (if things go right) for VG to grow into the LNG profile. Both are in a great business. The growth profile is clearly reflected in the forward EV/EBITDA, where the company’s trade close at 10x and 12x. Meanwhile, current EV/EBITDAs are 24.5x and 11.5x. It is also borne out by the higher EV/sales and P/sales metrics.

The balance sheet shows both have very high debt levels. However, this is supported by the business model, which is to sell production (for the most part) through long-term contracts (20-years). The latest data is VG having long-term contracts for 39.25 MTPA.

I expect many more will be added to that soon, as management said the following on the latest earnings call:

We're very active in a significant number of negotiations for long-term contracts at this point, mostly all 20-year terms. And so we expect to be executing and reporting on multiple 20-year contracts in the incoming quarters. And we're really pleased with the demand and where we stand in those negotiations. So, we -- the market, the investors in our company should expect to see announcements for more 20-year deals.

Europe has been cutting back its reliance on Russian gas. In 2021, it got 45% of its gas from Russia. That’s down to 19%. The current roadmap to fully cut dependency on Russian gas plans to cut this 19% down by another 1/3rd at the end of this year. Then it is supposed to go to zero by the end of 2027. LNG is a growth market to begin with but this should be a significant tailwind to the industry and Europe is likely to embrace LNG imports as these support the desire to cut reliance on Russia but also help with the trade tensions between Europe and the U.S. VG could ultimately exceed LNG's export capacity. LNG is a growth market and Europe is desperate to replace Russian supply. There is still quite a way to go before VG is valued in-line with Cheniere while they are very comparable. Even though the stock has strongly responded to the news events, I believe it is a strong buy for now while it narrows this valuation gap.

data.title: Nike: Moderate Upside Driven By Innovation And Pricing Power
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Josh Lukimin as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Nike, Inc. (NYSE:NKE) has a moderate upside over a five-year horizon, based on my DCF valuation. My base-case DCF suggests a target price of $63.32 per share (vs. $61.57 as of 3 Jun 2025, implying a 2.8% upside), underpinned by Nike’s strong brand momentum and innovation pipeline. I issue a Buy recommendation on Nike, representing confidence in the company's ability to deliver mid-single-digit revenue growth and margin expansion through FY2029. On balance, Nike’s competitive advantages and resilient demand supports this rating, even though investors should monitor rising risks from geopolitical tariffs and local competition.

As transitory headwinds (excess inventory, China lockdowns) abate, I expect a return to steady growth. The investment thesis is that Nike’s has a strong brand equity, direct consumer engagement, and global scale, all of which will help in yielding sustainable earnings growth which justifies my valuation. I have also taken conservative assumptions post-2025 in light of recent guidance misses. Overall, the analysis sees Nike as a high-quality franchise with moderate upside potential which is suitable for long-term investors.

Nike - Business Analysis

As the market leader, Nike can capitalize on the “Global athleisure” trend with its broad product portfolio and cultural relevance. Its women’s and lifestyle lines such as Air Jordan and Converse tap into both the fashion and function markets. Product innovation such as Flyknit materials and Air cushioning also helps keep Nike at the forefront of consumer preferences. More importantly, Nike maintains pricing power due to its brand prestige. Recently, it has been able to implement incremental price hikes (e.g. +$5–$10 on popular footwear) with limited pushback, which helps to offset inflation in input costs. Nike’s FY2023 gross margin was also aided by “strategic pricing actions” even with higher freight and materials costs. This ability to pass on costs shows strong consumer loyalty and product differentiation, supporting the margin expansion assumptions used in the model.

Note: Nike’s financial forecast through 2029 has been modeled under three scenarios, with the base case forming the primary valuation. All scenarios use a five-year DCF framework (FY 2025–2029 explicit forecasts) and uses the terminal value in 2029. The key assumptions and justifications are as follows:

Revenue Growth

In the base case, I project Nike’s revenue to grow at roughly 5.57% CAGR from FY2025 to FY2029. This reflects Nike’s strong brand and new growth avenues, tempered by competition and market maturity. A 5.57% CAGR is slightly above global GDP growth and in line with the sportswear industry’s expansion, and I have calculated it from its historical revenue from 2019 ($39,117 million) - 2024 ($51,362 million). In dollar terms, Nike’s sales would rise from ~$51 billion in FY2024 to around $68–$70 billion by FY2029 in the base scenario. For the Bullish Scenario, it has a higher end revenue growth of 6% CAGR if conditions are ideal. Examples include China re-accelerating toward double-digit growth, Nike managing to capture outsized market share in key categories or new product lines (like women’s apparel or connected fitness products) which can add incremental sales. This would increase FY2029 revenue by roughly $300 million.

For the bearish scenario, a lower end growth of 5% CAGR might occur if macro or competitive pressures hits. Examples include a US/Europe recession that slows demand, or Nike losing share in China such that global sales barely outpace inflation. In that case, FY2029 revenue might fall by $300 million from the base case, which reduces upside. In the last decade, Nike’s lowest annual growth periods were around 0–5% (during recession or the pandemic), so even the bear case assumes Nike remains growth-positive, on average.

Profit Margins

Nike’s EBIT margin in FY2024 was ~12.29%, due to pressures like elevated freight costs and markdowns. I forecast margin improvement in the long term as these pressures ease and the DTC strategy pays off. In the base case, we model EBIT margin rising to ~11% by 2029, which implies Nike recaptures some operating leverage and benefits from a richer gross margin. Specifically, gross margin could improve due to a higher proportion of sales via Nike Direct (which carry higher product margins), and product mix shift to premium innovations. Meanwhile, we expect Nike to continue investing in marketing and innovation, which enables SG&A to grow in line with revenue. Our margin outlook is supported by industry trends such as DTC businesses that tend to have higher net margins once scale is achieved.

Capital Expenditures & Investments

Nike’s business model is capital-light relative to its revenue as it outsources manufacturing and rents many of its retail stores, which means that capex goes mainly into corporate facilities, distribution centers, tech infrastructure, and owned store build-outs. Over FY2020–2024, Nike’s capex averaged only ~1.9% of revenue. In our model, we assume capital expenditures continue at ~2% of sales in the base case, roughly tracking revenue growth in absolute dollars. This level should be sufficient for Nike’s needs without constraining free cash flow.

Nike - Valuation

We use a discount rate (WACC) of ~8.5% in our DCF, reflecting Nike’s low debt and equity risk profile. Nike has a strong balance sheet (minimal net debt) and a beta of 1.23. We set the terminal growth rate at 2.5%, reflecting a conservative long-term growth slightly above inflation, justified by Nike’s ongoing brand strength. We calculate this by taking Nike’s current outstanding bonds ($7,631 million), with the tax-shield of 16% derived from their income before taxes and tax expense. Our DCF output for the base case yields an equity value per share around $63.32, which is moderately above the current market price, indicating the stock is mildly undervalued. For completeness, the bull case DCF (higher growth, higher margins) gives a value in the $75.84 range, while the bear case DCF (slower growth, margin pressure) gives a value of around $58.88. This range also aligns with the current market sentiment of $61 where Nike stock appears to be pricing in a degree of caution (somewhere between our base and bear cases, likely due to recent growth concerns in China and global demand). Should Nike execute well and deliver per our base or bull case, there is potential for the stock to rise higher.

Risks to Thesis

China Market Underperformance

If China sales remain flat or contract, FY 2025–2029 revenue CAGR could fall to 4% instead of 5%. This directly reduces the projected free cash flows by 5–7% annually. Lower cash flow forecasts through 2029 would drive down the present value of the forecast period. It also reduces terminal-year revenue by approximately $2.5–$3 billion. Re-running the DCF with an even lower CAGR could further lower our base-case equity value and potentially eliminate the perceived upside.

Intensifying Competition and Market Saturation

Digitally native upstarts have eroded some of Nike’s historic advantages in the price‐sensitive and trend‐driven segments of athleisure. Brands like Allbirds, On Running, and Rothy’s have built online‐first distribution models and leveraged social media to cultivate a strong following. Their DTC platforms allow them to keep price points 10–20% below Nike’s entry‐level offerings. Fast‐fashion giants such as Zara and H&M are also releasing “sport‐inspired” capsule collections at aggressive price tiers, trying to undercut Nike’s mid‐tier athleisure lines. If these nimble competitors continue to grab market share in the $50–$120 price bracket where Nike has historically been strongest, Nike’s assumed 5-6% revenue CAGR could slip even more.

Tariff shocks leading to higher WACC & lower terminal growth

In 2018–2019, the Trump administration imposed tariffs on a wide array of Chinese imports, ranging from footwear to textiles. Nike sources a large portion of its finished goods and raw materials from China, and thus experienced an uptick in freight and duty costs. Even though Nike managed to pass a portion of these higher costs onto consumers, it still absorbed enough expense to decrease gross margins by around 0.75% in FY 2019–FY 2020. For our DCF model, if we assume a tariff shock similar to 2018, it directly lowers our forecasted EBIT by $150–$200 million each year until tariffs subside. Additionally, heightened trade tensions lead to elevated volatility in equities and increasing the risk premium. If Nike’s beta increases from 1.23 towards 1.30–1.35 in a prolonged tariff environment, the equity risk premium component of WACC could rise by 0.25%-0.50%. Rerunning this DCF model, even a 0.30% increase in WACC can reduce the present value of five years of FCF by ~4–5% and decrease the terminal value by 6%.

Final Recommendation

Nike, Inc. is a globally dominant athletic brand with robust long-term growth prospects, and our DCF valuation points to a moderate upside from the current share price. The company’s strengths include having iconic brand equity, a successful DTC pivot, innovation in product and sustainability, and broad-based global exposure which form a compelling investment thesis for steady value creation. While the upside in our base scenario is not explosive, it is solid enough for a high-quality lower-risk equity like Nike.

data.title: SS&C Technologies Delivers On Execution - But Can It Deliver On Valuation?
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Over the past decade, SS&C Technologies Holdings Inc. (SSNC) has evolved from a niche software vendor into a technology-enabled operations platform with industry-leading EBIT margins and the strongest EPS and ROIC growth rates among peers. However, this growth has come at a cost. The company’s reliance on acquisitions has resulted in a persistently high Reinvestment rate and a stretched financial position. While the valuation suggests a 20% margin of safety, it was based on very optimistic assumptions. As such, I would not consider this an investment opportunity.

Company Background

SS&C is a global provider of software and software-enabled services primarily for the financial services and healthcare industries. It enables these organizations to outsource complex operational functions while leveraging advanced software systems to improve efficiency, compliance, and performance.

The company derives its revenue from 2 sources:

  • Software-enabled services. This is the bigger revenue contributor, accounting for 82% of the 2024 total revenue. Refer to the left part of Chart 1.
  • License, maintenance, and related. These are primarily from perpetual license fees, maintenance fees, and professional services.

The company derives a big part of its revenue from the US. In 2024, the US accounted for about 69% of the total revenue. Refer to the right part of Chart 2.

Over the past decade, SS&C’s business model has evolved significantly, transforming from a product-centric software provider into a service-centric, technology-enabled operations partner.

  • The company initially focused on delivering proprietary software to asset and wealth managers. However, through major acquisitions, SS&C has expanded its capabilities and diversified its client base, entering new verticals such as healthcare, insurance, and retirement services.
  • Over time, SS&C has also integrated its portfolio into a unified, cloud-enabled ecosystem that combines software, data analytics, and outsourced operations. This evolution has empowered clients to streamline workflows, manage regulatory complexity, and offload non-core functions.

The technology-enabled services space is constantly evolving. SS&C’s track record of adapting to change provides confidence that it is well-positioned to evolve alongside future technological developments.

SS&C operates in 3 key sectors - financial technology (fintech), business process outsourcing [BPO], and healthcare IT. These are growing sectors as exemplified by the following:

“The global fintech market was valued at USD 340.10 billion in 2024…exhibiting a CAGR of 16.2% during the forecast period (2025 to 2032). North America dominated the global market with a share of 34.05% in 2024.” Fortune Business Insights

“The global business process outsourcing market size was estimated at USD 302.62 billion in 2024 and is anticipated to grow at a CAGR of 9.8% from 2025 to 2030.” Grand View Research

“The healthcare technology market size has grown rapidly in recent years. It will grow from $507.25 billion in 2024… It will grow…2029… at a compound annual growth rate [CAGR] of 20.6%.” The Business Research Company

Acquisition-led Growth

Over the past decade, the company's revenue grew at a 22% CAGR. A big part of this growth was due to acquisitions.

You can get a sense of this by comparing the amount spent on CAPEX and acquisitions over the past decade. During this period, the company incurred USD 1.2 billion on CAPEX (for organic growth) compared to USD 12.8 billion on acquisitions.

While this is an inferred back-of-the-envelope analysis, it suggests that less than 10% of the revenue growth was due to organic growth.

As shown in the right part of Chart 2, a significant part of the revenue growth was in 2018/2019. While the company reported acquisitions almost every year, in 2018, it spent about USD 7 billion to acquire DST Systems, Eze Software, Intralinks, and CACEIS North America, which contributed about half of the 2018 revenue.

More importantly, the company was able to multiply the revenue growth into a higher profit growth. Over the past decade, PAT grew at 38% CAGR. This higher PAT growth rate came partly from the higher revenue and partly because of gross profit margin expansion as shown in the left part of Chart 2.

At the same time, it was able to control its Selling, General and Administration [SGA] expenses so that the SGA margin in 2024 was lower than that in 2015. You can see from the right part of Chart 2 that fixed cost was also well controlled with the fixed cost margin in 2024 of 35%, down from the 45% in 2015.

Notes to Chart 2.

a. I broke down the operating profits into fixed costs and variable costs.

  • Fixed cost = SGA, R&D and Depreciation & Amortization.
  • Variable cost = Cost of Sales – Depreciation & Amortization.
  • Contribution = Revenue – Variable Cost.
  • Contribution margin = Contribution/Revenue.

b. Note the declining contribution margin given the improving gross profit margin. This was because of the way I defined the variable cost. Depreciation & Amortization had reduced compared to the increase in the other components of the cost of sales. So, when I excluded the Depreciation & Amortization in deriving the contribution, we had the declining contribution margin.

The improving profits led to improved returns. From 2015 to 2024, ROIC went from 2.6% to 8.6% while ROE increased from 2% to 11.5%.

With the current WACC of 8.1% and cost of equity of 8.8%, I must say that it was only in the past few years that we had a situation where the returns were greater than the respective cost of funds, meaning that shareholders’ value was created only in recent years.

Reinvestment Challenges

Growth needs to be funded, and one metric for this is the Reinvestment rate, defined as Reinvestment/NOPAT.

Reinvestment = CAPEX & Acquisition – Depreciation & Amortization + increase in Net Working Capital

Over the past decade, SS&C had an average Reinvestment rate of 146%. This is an unsustainable rate. A big part of this was due to the acquisitions. If I ignore acquisitions, the Reinvestment rate drops to negative, as the amount spent on CAPEX was more than offset by the Depreciation & Amortization.

The company has stated that it will “continue to capitalize on acquisitions of complementary businesses and technologies”.

To bring this to a rate significantly below 100% while continuing with its acquisition plan, the company can either increase the NOPAT or become more cost-effective in acquiring companies.

Looking at its historical results, I think that there is room to increase the profits. This is especially when its peers (refer to the Peer comparison section) were able to deliver much better return on capital.

I would think that the company should also focus on improving its acquisitions through either lower acquisition prices or higher synergies. If you stand back and look, the company incurred USD 12.8 billion over the past decade on acquisitions while annual profit went from USD 43 million in 2015 to USD 762 million in 2024

Taking a very optimistic view of an increase of USD 719 million per year, the USD 12.8 billion is equal to about 18 years' payback. Sure, it is a simplistic view, but this is assuming that the company can achieve USD 719 million improvements annually from 2015. My point is that this is a long payback period and suggests areas to focus on, i.e., corporate M&A excellence rather than operating excellence only.

Over the long run, I would expect the Reinvestment rate to be governed by the fundamental growth equation.

Growth = Return X Reinvestment rate.

Assuming a 20% growth and a 60% Reinvestment rate, the Return should be 33%. You can see the challenge for the company. But I think there are signs that this could be achieved, as over the past decade, there have been signs of improving capital efficiency.

  • Gross profitability improved from 8% in 2015 to 15% in 2024.
  • Asset turnover improved from 17% in 2015 to 31% in 2024.

Peer Comparison

I compared SS&C's performance with the following listed peers serving the same sectors:

  • Broadridge Financial Solutions Inc. (BR). A provider of investor communications and technology-driven solutions for wealth and asset management, proxy voting, and financial operations.
  • Cognizant Technology Solutions Corporation (CTSH). A leading global provider of information technology, consulting, and business process outsourcing services. Its services span various industries, including financial services and healthcare.
  • Fidelity National Information Services, Inc. (FIS). FIS is a global financial technology company that provides banking and payment technologies, asset and wealth management platforms, and outsourced services to financial institutions.

As shown in Table 1, SS&C is the smallest in terms of the 2024 revenue, but it had the best revenue growth rate due to its acquisitions.

I compared SS&C’s 2015 to 2024 performances with those of its peers based on the following metrics. Refer to Charts 3 and 4.

Overall, SS&C distinguished itself not by starting at the top, but by outperforming peers in margin quality and upward momentum across key shareholder value metrics.

I would consider SS&C delivering one of the strongest improvement trajectories among its peers, combining industry-leading EBIT margins and consistent gains in capital efficiency and earnings quality.

  • While it ranked in the lower half by absolute return on capital and EPS levels, it stood out with the best growth trends in both metrics over the past decade.
  • Its EBIT margin was the highest in the peer group, underscoring operational discipline. Although its free cash flow margin slipped slightly, it still maintained a strong No. 2 position in 2024.
  • In terms of quantum, SS&C was in the lower half of the EPS performance. But it had the best EPS growth rate over the past decade.

SS&C’s improving fundamentals - margin leadership, accelerating capital efficiency, and sustained FCF performance - position it well for future outperformance. This is especially if it can continue to scale EPS and grow its return on capital to peer-leading levels. In a peer group where many are mature and margin-constrained, SS&C stands out.

Financial Position

I have concerns about SS&C's financial position. There are several challenging points:

  • As of the end of March 2025, it has USD 515 million in cash, equal to about 3% of its total assets.
  • It also had a Debt-to-Equity ratio of 103%. While high, it has come down from its 2018 peak of 180%.
  • I have already mentioned its unsustainable Reinvestment rate.
  • It had a challenging capital allocation plan, as shown in Table 2. You can see that the cash flow from operations was not enough to fund its CAPEX and acquisitions. It had to rely on new shares and/or increased borrowings.

Its main positive point is that over the past decade, it generated positive cash flow from operations every year with a good cash flow conversion rate. During this period, it generated a total of USD 9.4 billion in cash flow from operations compared to the total PAT of USD 4.5 billion.

However, until the company can improve its Reinvestment rate or improve its organic growth rate (to reduce its reliance on acquisitions), it would be tough to improve its financial position.

Valuation

My analysis showed that SS&C is on a path to improve its profitability. Apart from the improving returns, there are signs of better operating and capital efficiencies, such a better gross profit margins, lower SGA margins, better gross profitability, and improving asset turnover.

In the immediate term, acquisition will still have a big role in this. While I have concerns about its financial position, I assumed that improving performance would provide some breathing space to improve its financial position.

As such, I adopted the following positive picture in valuing the company.

  • The base revenue would be the 2024 revenue growing at 20% in Year 1. I based this on the past decade's growth rate, assuming a continuation of the acquisition plans. This growth rate would be reduced proportionately over the next 5 years to the terminal 4% growth rate.
  • The base contribution margin would be the 2019 to 2024 average values (post the significant acquisitions in 2018). This would improve proportionately to be 10% better in the terminal year.
  • The base capital efficiency would be the average 2019 to 2024 value.
  • The base Reinvestment rate would be the past decades' average values and reduced proportionately to one based on the fundamental growth equation in the terminal year.
  • The fixed cost would be pegged to the average 2019 to 2024 fixed cost margin. I also assume that there would be improved cost control so that in the terminal year, the fixed cost margin would be 90% of the base fixed cost margin.
  • The tax rate would be the past decade's average tax rate.

On such a basis, I obtained an intrinsic value of USD 96 per share compared to its market price (4 Jun 2025) of USD 80 per share. While there is a 20% margin of safety, it is below my 30% target.

Valuation model

I valued SS&C based on a multi-stage Free Cash Flow to the Firm [FCFF] model, as shown in Table 3.

The basic equations used in the model are:

FCFF = EBIT(1 – t) – Reinvestment.

EBIT = Revenue X Contribution margin – Fixed cost

The contribution margin and fixed cost are based on the operating model shown in the right part of Chart 2.

The Reinvestment was based on the Reinvestment margin.

The fixed cost was based on the fixed cost margin.

Notes to Table 3.

a. Proportionate change

b. Pegged to the growth rate

c. Assumed proportionate change

d. Assumed proportionate change

e. Revenue X Margin, and after accounting for Fixed costs

f. Assumed proportionate change

g. b X f

h. FCFF for each year = e - g

j. Refer to the WACC table

k. NPV for each year = (h X j)

l. Terminal for the year discounted at the terminal growth rate

m. 5 years NPV + terminal value

n. Inclusive of any excess TCE. Non-operating assets, MI, and Debt

o. Based on the number of shares

The WACC used in the valuation model was based on the first page results of a Google search for “SS&C Technologies WACC”. Refer to Table 4.

Valuation Limitations and Risks

The main concern I have is my assumption about the decreasing Reinvestment rate.

This will mean that the company improves its M&A capabilities so that it acquires companies at a lower payback period.

If you look at the valuation equations, the Reinvestment impacts the free cash flow that, in turn, affects the value of the business. If the Reinvestment cannot be reduced, the intrinsic value would be lower.

With this optimistic Reinvestment picture in the background, I would think that, as a whole, my valuation assumptions are on a very optimistic side. This is because in the terminal year:

  • The projected return on capital would be 11% compared to the past 3 years' average return of 7%.
  • The projected EBIT margin in the terminal year of 32% would be off the scale in the peer EBIT margin chart. Refer to the right part of Chart 3.
  • Its projected EPS would be USD 11.67 per share compared to the 2024 USD 3.09 EPS. Based on the peer performance, the terminal EPS would also be off the peer chart. Refer to the right part of Chart 4.

My view is that the peers provide some form of reality check. You can thus understand why I would not consider SS&C an investment opportunity. The 20% margin of safety is based on a very optimistic set of assumptions.

Conclusion

SS&C may not be the largest player in the fintech and BPO space, but over the past decade, it delivered one of the strongest operational improvements in its peer group.

Through acquisitions, the company has transformed from a niche software vendor into a technology-enabled services platform with industry-leading EBIT margins and improving capital efficiency. While its earnings per share were modest in absolute terms, their upward trajectory stands out.

Much of SS&C’s historical growth has been acquisition-driven. But the company was able to use the revenue growth to expand the gross profit margins while controlling SGA and fixed costs. Operationally, there were improvements in operating and capital efficiencies.

But there are some concerns. Apart from the low organic growth rate, the other concerns are the unsustainable Reinvestment rate and financial position.

While my valuation provides a 20% margin of safety, it is based on a very optimistic set of assumptions. Given the concerns and the valuation picture, I would not consider this an investment opportunity from a long-term value investment perspective.

data.title: Helen of Troy: Ignore The Seemingly Good Value
data.description:
Ugly Portrait

We agree that Helen of Troy Ltd. (HELE) deserves the strong sell Quant Rating assigned to the stock by Seeking Alpha. We do not foresee any catalyst for a turnaround. The shortfall in revenue and earnings, the worrisome cash flow from operations, and the reduced cash on hand since the end of FY ’22 are likely to exacerbate, in our opinion. Internal problems for the company, trade, and supply issues are going to linger and hurt any turnaround effort. Management is offering no guidance for FY '26, dampening investors’ interests and essentially stifling any uptick in momentum. There is so little interest and trading in Helen of Troy that the Levered/Unlevered Beta is at 0.19.

Complicating hopes for a turnaround after the shares dived -85% over the previous 5 years, down 73.4% over the last 12 months, and -53.35% YTD, is the turmoil in the C-suite marked by the abrupt departure of Helen’s CEO “effective immediately,” per the May 2, ’25 announcement. Adding to the negativity is Seeking Alpha’s recent red notice warning highlighting Helen’s declining growth and “The company has CAPEX Growth (YoY) of -50.1% while the Consumer Discretionary sector median is -3.33%.” 13 out of 13 metrics for growth graded by Seeking Alpha for growth get near failing grade.

The kerfuffle between nations is causing uncertain times for Helen of Troy and the stock. The former CEO told shareholders America’s global trade policy is causing “significant uncertainty and disruption to our business.” Helen needs to “diversify its supply chain and reduce costs,” move its home and outdoor, beauty, and wellness consumer products manufacturing out of China to “mitigate 70%-80% of the tariff impacts…”

Helen of Troy lost its mojo as it worked its way out of the pandemic years. The stock was approximately $240 per share at the close of 2021, then dropped 100 points over the year to close 2023 at about $140 per share. Revenue reportedly fell $2.22B to $2B, and for 2025 is expected to be around $1.9B. Gross profit was $953.2M in 2022 but will just top $914M in 2025. Earnings from continuing operations will be less than half in 2025 ($123.8M) of what they were in 2022 ($253.9M). Cash and receivables are also less than in previous years, while long-term debt is up from $659.4M at the end of 2024 to ~$917.5M at the last report. Even Helen’s most recent acquisition, Olive and June, had declining sales as reported in Q4 ’25. Despite the recent share price rise of ~18% during the first week of June ’25, the short interest ratio tops 11%; days to cover dropped from about 5 in March to 2.8 on May 15. By comparison, shorts had 15.8 days to cover on June 14, ’24.

Valuation

On September ’24, we rated the stock a speculative buy at about $59 per share. It subsequently climbed to +$74 each. 9 months later, we figure a fair price to be about $28 per share by multiplying the PE of 5.13 times a forecast $5.44 EPS for FY ’25. Using the FY ’24 EPS figure, a fair price for the stock would be $36.7. Ours is on the high end of speculative EPS among analysts. Analysts using various Discounted Cash Flow models and exits peg Helen of Troy’s intrinsic value from $43 to $119 per share.

Risks

The intrinsic valuation might help determine a fair price for a sugar daddy interested in acquiring the brand names, Helen’s greatest assets, plus its retail outlets and the success of management’s investments in online sales. In our pessimistic opinion, the shares will move between the mid-to-high $20s for a long time to come, while the PE hovers at a low 5.13, the supply chain is threatened, and future sales and earnings forecasts are on hold. Other problems the company faces include finding brand names that ensure a growth platform.

Olive and June's sales were less than expected per the last earnings report. The brand marketing of beauty products is changing with outreach to Gen Z. They are susceptible to influencers, product safety, and, as one observer put it to Fairchild Media, “getting a good deal.” Consumer spending is still rising, but so are retail prices, as producers are passing along the cost of tariffs. These conditions have the potential to hurt margins or increase inflation, which, in either scenario, does not bode well for Helen of Troy over the next few quarters or longer.

Revlon emerged from bankruptcy in 2023; can lenders who took over and are reorganizing the beauty business build the brand the way marketing and make-up specialists did? Please note that Olive and June’s subscription membership, trending nail online shopping logo, is not among the brands promoted on Helen’s website. The Helen management must be able to mitigate consumer loss risks, first, to dividend-paying stocks and in the field to cheaper knock-offs. Finally, Helen pays no dividend, and in uncertain times, that is what retail value investors consider when investing. The next earnings report is scheduled to be announced on July 8, 2025.

There is some risk in adopting a strong sell judgment when the stock is low-priced. Insiders made 37 open-market buys this past year, with few sales reported. Hedge funds made small trades in the last three months, but fewer funds own Helen of Troy stock in 2025 than in the previous 5 quarters.

Debt presents another risk to be considered as a worrisome risk. The debt-to-equity ratio is 54.5% and has increased from 30% over 5 years. Operating cash flow does not cover the debt payments, but EBIT does cover the debt.

Takeaway

Helen of Troy is a profitable company, and its stock price is enticing, but it deserves a strong sell rating for retail value investors. Buying shares at this good valuation is risky because of the intrinsic and extrinsic turmoil. Neither insiders nor funds are flocking to the stock.

Margins are higher than for the Consumer Discretion sector median, except for cash per share and the asset turnover ratio. Growth potential and momentum are keeping a lid on the share price, and since the firm pays no dividend, this is not a worthwhile investment for retail value investors. The share price descent cannot be turned around quickly and without pain. In December ’21, the company’s market cap touched $6B, while it currently figures to be $633M. We believe the company is liquid enough to cover liabilities when they are due, but, in our opinion, the investment it will take in marketing the brand names to new generations of consumers is going to be a hefty undertaking.

data.title: GoodRx: Stabilizing Revenue Amid Healthy Profit Gains
data.description:
Though the stock market has continued to glide upward and recoup the losses since Trump’s tariffs were announced, I continue to believe that dispersion will increase - particularly, that the large-cap stocks that have dominated the market over the last few years will see a correction in their valuations, while value stocks in the small cap space shine after years of underperformance.

GoodRx (NASDAQ:GDRX), a prescription drug savings company, is a great and little-known small cap value stock. Under new leadership, the company is adapting its go-to market playbook and stabilizing its revenue, while continuing to grow adjusted EBITDA. Down ~10% since the start of January and ~50% over the trailing twelve months, investors certainly need patience here - but this is a great stock to bank on for a rebound.

I last wrote a buy opinion on GoodRx in March, upgrading it from a prior neutral rating when it was trading in the mid-$4s. Since then, the company has slightly accelerated its pace of revenue growth, buoyed by its strategy of forging direct relationships with pharmaceutical companies. It has also increased its full-year adjusted EBITDA outlook. Amid its declining valuation, I’m reiterating my buy rating on this stock.

In my view, these are the core reasons to be bullish on GoodRx:

  • GoodRx’s revenue base can almost be considered recurring. Since the company primarily deals in subscription drugs, many consumers who utilize GoodRx promotions will keep coming back to refill their prescriptions, earning the company a recurring slice of that transaction.
  • Broadening distribution channels. The company’s sales from its pivot to direct pharmaceutical relationships are growing in the double digits. This channel gives GoodRx access to a new set of consumers and at favorable margins.
  • Compelling adjusted EBITDA margins. Even amid weaker revenue growth, GoodRx is managing to maintain mid-30s adjusted EBITDA margins and generating consistent profitability gains.
  • Potential buyout candidate. Though I never base an investment decision solely on a company’s potential to be acquired, GoodRx has all the right traits of a company fit to be acquired: a small and undemanding market cap, rich adjusted EBITDA, and a niche business with a recurring clientele.

Perhaps the biggest advantage to investing in this stock, of course, is its cheap price: at current share prices near $4, GoodRx trades at a market cap of $1.51 billion. After we net off the $301.0 million of cash and $485.8 million of debt on GoodRx’s latest balance sheet, the company’s resulting enterprise value is $1.69 billion.

For FY25, GoodRx has maintained its full-year revenue outlook at $810-$840 million, or 2-6% growth. However, it has slightly nudged up its adjusted EBITDA guidance to $273-$287 million, the midpoint of which implies a 33.9% margin.

This puts GoodRx’s valuation multiples at:

  • 2.0x EV/FY25 revenue
  • 6.0x EV/FY25 adjusted EBITDA

GoodRx’s 6x adjusted EBITDA multiple is more appropriate to a company that is at risk of seeing revenue declines or has a major headwind to margins - neither of which apply here. While acknowledging that the company is going through a challenging business transition, investors still have an opportunity to buy into this stock for a healthy rebound.

Q1 download

Let’s now go through GoodRx’s latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q1 earnings summary is shown below:

GoodRx’s revenue grew 2.6% y/y to $203.0 million, ahead of Wall Street’s $202.2 million (+2.2% y/y) expectations. Revenue growth also accelerated meaningfully against 1.0% growth in Q4.

That being said, we acknowledge that it wasn’t a perfect quarter. One of the more worrying aspects within Q1 was the fact that the company’s count of monthly active consumers (MACS) dipped again to 6.4 million, after briefly rising to 6.6 million in Q4 and sparking hopes of stabilization. Subscription plans, however, did hold relatively steady at ~680k, primarily GoodRx Gold members as the company has now largely lapped the headwind of ending the Kroger Savings plan.

Another major possible disruption for GoodRx is the recent bankruptcy announcement of Rite Aid. Though the company is expected to sell off many of its locations as operational pharmacies, inevitably some locations will shutter, capping off GoodRx’s access to some of its consumer base. Per CEO Wendy Barnes’ remarks on the Q1 earnings call:

I want to acknowledge the ongoing uncertainty in the macroeconomic environment driven by factors that could influence our business, including regulatory and policy changes, shifting consumer sentiment, tariffs and evolving government initiatives. I also want to acknowledge the news pertaining to the Rite Aid bankruptcy announcement this week. This is recent news and we are actively working to understand more. What we know today is that they have reentered bankruptcy and are pursuing a sale of substantially all of their assets. It is too early to know how this will exactly play out, but we do believe that we are well positioned to engage consumers and other pharmacy partners to ensure smooth patient transitions. Ultimately, these prescriptions will be filled somewhere and we are in active dialogue with pharmacy partners to make sure we are best positioned to service our consumers.

I would also highlight that our efforts to broadly engage pharmacy partners over the past several months have put us in a strong position for productive dialogue with potential buyers or landing spots for these patients, and we believe that we can create unique and mutually beneficial arrangements to support such patient transitions. Despite these potential challenges, the strength of the GoodRx brand remains clear across all economic conditions.”

This only serves to highlight the importance of GoodRx’s shift to direct pharmaceutical marketing. In Q1, pharma manufacturer solutions revenue grew 17% y/y to $28.6 million, now representing ~15% of total revenue and helping to offset the y/y headwinds from a smaller subscription headcount.

GoodRx’s adjusted EBITDA also rose 11% y/y to $69.8 million, with margins rising 270bps y/y to 34.4%.

The company continues to be particularly fierce on opex management, in particular cutting sales and marketing expenses down -4% y/y (and shrinking it by 2 points as a percentage of revenue).

Risks and key takeaways

There are, of course, a minefield of risks that GoodRx investors need to consumer. The retail pharmacy business has been weak since COVID, and the bankruptcy of Rite Aid not only serves to demonstrate those challenges but also cuts off a portion of GoodRx’s customer base. The company is growing double digits via its pharmacy relationships, but so far this is still a minor portion of revenue compared to its current transactional cuts that it earns from consumers redeeming coupons in-store.

That being said, I think these risks are already more than priced into GoodRx’s stock at ~6x adjusted EBITDA. Buy here and hold out for a rebound.

data.title: Shoe Carnival: Buy This Small Cap For Exceptional Dividend Growth
data.description:
Investment Thesis

After losing 50% of its value over the last year, Shoe Carnival (NASDAQ:SCVL) is now a great buying opportunity for dividend investors looking to diversify their portfolio with an under-the-radar small cap. The company has managed to grow its dividend distribution at a compound annual growth rate of 28% over the last five years, giving investors a 5-year yield on cost of 9.95%.

With a strong balance sheet and low payout ratio, investors can feel secure knowing Shoe Carnival's 12 years of consecutive dividend payments will continue to grow.

Company Overview

Market Cap

$532.1M

5-Year Revenue Growth

4.88%

Enterprise Value

$804.5M

Return on Equity

10.52%

12-month Return (%)

-50.78%

Price/Book

0.81x

Inside Ownership

31.60%

Price/Cash Flow

7.01x

Over the last year, Shoe Carnival's stock has been hit hard - the stock price is down over 50% while the earnings are only slightly down from the peak of 2021. Aside from the current cyclical downtrend in the family retail space, there are several different investment themes taking place that have caused investors to reject the stock.

First, and most significant, the tariff announcement has had a big impact on the retail sector. Morgan Stanley estimates that retail will be the worst performing sector if the trade dispute is not resolved. On average, the investment bank expects that retailers could see as much as a 35% hit to earnings per share.

Looking beyond tariffs, investors have shunned small-cap opportunities. With the recent performance of the Mag-7, investors are migrating out of small-cap stocks to larger, more 'glamorous' large and mega caps.

The chart above from the CFA Society Blog highlights the underperformance in small caps relative to large caps - a 12-year trend that reverts during periods of high inflation or uncertainty.

The last investment theme working against Shoe Carnival is the underperformance of value vs. growth stocks. SCVL is a value stock, which as an investment theme has become out of favor in long and extended bull markets. The CFA Society publication highlights that value stocks have historically outperformed - like small caps - during periods of high interest rates and market turmoil.

As a small-cap value stock, Shoe Carnival is currently out of favor with the investing community, driven by the themes from the extended bull market mentioned above. Investors willing to look past the negative view on the company have an opportunity to buy a discounted dividend grower with upside appreciation built in.

Operations

Shoe Carnival is a discounted family footwear retailer operating primarily in the Midwest. The company sells lower-cost athletic and casual footwear across 429 stores. Of the 429 total stores, SCVL operates 334 Shoe Carnival stores, 67 Shoe Station stores, and 28 Rogan's Shoes stores.

Per the abbreviated income statement above, Shoe Carnival has grown revenue at a compound annual growth rate of 3.9% from 2016 to 2022.

The company had several years of flat to declining revenue until its late-2021 acquisition of Shoe Station. The acquisition proved to be a windfall for the company - operating margins in 2021 and 2022 were 16.0% and 11.6%, respectively. Through internal sales data, management identified that Shoe Station, a premium brand compared to Shoe Carnival, offers greater sales and profitability opportunities. Specifically, in SCVL's most recent earnings call, management said that Shoe Station is the fastest growing retailer over the last two years.

The company has since embarked on a multi-year 'rebanner' strategy, which is intended to rebrand the existing Shoe Carnival stores into Shoe Station stores. The program, first run as a pilot in 2022, is now expected to be complete by the end of 2027 as management plans to rebanner at least 80% of its Shoe Carnival stores. With several stores already under the new branding, Shoe Carnival recorded 4.9% sales growth in its Shoe Station segment, while the broader retail footwear industry saw declining revenues. Despite strong growth in Shoe Station, Q1 2025 totals sales were down 10% for on the quarter, but management expects total sales to be down only -2% for the year.

Peer Performance

In the table below I have included several of Shoe Carnival's peers, including American Eagle Outfitters (AEO), Guess?, Inc (GES), Genesco (GCO), and Designer Brands (DBI) for a comparison of growth, profitability, and valuation.

The average and median growth rates across the sector have been very meager. The median 3-year revenue growth rate is -1.4% while SCVL is 190bps lower at -3.3%.

However, you'll notice in the abbreviated income statement (shown previously) that Shoe Carnival had incredible operating performance in 2021 and 2022. Per management, Shoe Carnival's 2021 acquisition of Shoe Station led to its best operating year in history.

As the company now enters its strategic rebanner program, revenues will be down relative to the 2021/2022 peak but are expected to rebound by 2027 after the program is completed. Despite the revenue underperformance, SCVL has an operating margin and net margin at 160bps and 370bps above peers, respectively.

The margin outperformance is driven by management's disciplined approach to managing expenses (like the ones highlighted in the table above) and maintaining a strong balance sheet.

Unlike so many post-acquisition transactions, management at Shoe Carnival has limited unnecessary costs and inefficiencies. Since the acquisition of Shoe Station, Shoe Carnival has been able to limit SG&A costs to a compound annual growth rate of only 3.39%. Additionally, it has maintained a 64% average cost of goods sold.

Highlighting the importance of a strong balance sheet, Shoe Carnival's net interest expense is only 0.03% of sales. In contrast with Guess? and Designer Brands, SCVL is able to generate a mid-single digit net income margin and convert almost all of its operating income to free cash flow.

Dividends

The dividend history graph above highlights the 10-year dividend performance and capital allocation strategy SCVL has committed to. Three main themes occur from the graph which include the yield, performance, and safety of SCVL's dividends.

First, because the stock has declined over 50% in the last year, the yield has jumped to a current rate of 3.08%. The dividend yield last peaked at 2.27% in April 2020 when retailers were getting crushed by the COVID-19 lockdown. Second, SCVL has consistently paid dividends for the last 12 years and has increased the yearly dividend at a compound annual growth rate of 17.7%. Although this number is already impressive enough, the company has increased the dividend at a compound annual growth rate of 28% over the last five years - if investors purchased SCVL five years ago, their yield would now be 9.95% on the original cost basis. Third, management's conservative approach to the dividend payout ratio ensures the dividend will be sustainable going forward.

The analysis below considers SCVL's dividend safety by looking at cash on hand and cash from operations, with a scenario for the Shoe Station rebanner and one for core operations.

The first component of dividend safety is the company's existing cash. As touched on previously, management has a very conservative balance sheet. The company has cash on hand of $68 million, which could pay the expected 2025 dividend four times over. Additionally, the company has no debt (if you exclude the operating leases), marking its 20th consecutive year of funding operations exclusively from cash flows.

The second component of dividend safety is Shoe Carnival's ability to generate cash flows. In the growth capex scenario, the trailing twelve-month cash from operations is $75.9 million, while the trailing twelve-month capital expenditures are $36.3 million.

This scenario accounts for the capital expenditures needed to continue the rebanner strategy. Capital expenditures are approximately $20 million higher, which brings free cash flow to $39.3 million. Under this scenario, the dividend is still covered 2.4x before considering the $68 million of cash on hand. The scenario which excludes growth capex is intended to highlight what SCVL's dividend coverage would look like under a 'normalized' scenario. Needless to say, with $20 million less spent on capital expenditures, the free cash flow covers the dividend 2.8x before considering cash on hand.

Valuation

Although the investment thesis is primarily focused on Shoe Carnival's ability to provide investors with reliable and growing dividends, the Shoe Station rebanner strategy presents a unique opportunity for upside growth.

In the table below, I have included several assumptions based on management's guidance used for the discounted cash flow model.

In an effort to be conservative and practical, I have assumed that net sales will decline by 3% for 2025 (where management expects only a 1-2% drop). Additionally, because of the ongoing rebanner strategy, I have increased the cost of sales by 130bps and increased SG&A costs 50bps.

In the income statement projections, my conservative estimate of revenue assumes a compound annual growth rate of only 0.72% from 2024 to 2028. Additionally, my 2025/2026 margins are slightly below the last few years of operating history, but rebound in 2027/2028 (and maintained at the terminal value in the next screenshot).

Based on these assumptions, I estimate a fair value of $21.80 for Shoe Carnival - a discount of 12.9% from its current price.

The investment strategy in this scenario is to buy Shoe Carnival under the assumption the dividends will continue to grow at a reasonable pace while maintaining adequate funding to support its distribution. At Shoe Carnival's current price, investors have the opportunity to purchase a company with a well-covered, growing dividend that offers $2 per share upside appreciation.

Risks

Rebanner Strategy

The primary risk that could impede Shoe Carnival's future cash flows is its rebanner strategy. As previously mentioned, the Shoe Station segment grew its revenue by 4.9% in the quarter and is considered the fastest growing retailer over the last two years. However, the rebanner strategy has not been implemented for a long enough time period to assure investors of its durability.

If the investment were to prove unsuccessful, it is likely the company's margins would be look more like the pre-Shoe Station acquisition. As highlighted previously, operating margins and net margins from the period 2016-2020 were significantly lower, which could impact Shoe Carnival's ability to generate substantial enough cash flow to cover its dividend. Although I have confidence in the rebanner strategy's success, it is something I will be paying close attention to over the next few quarters.

Tariffs

Shoe Carnival is susceptible to rising input costs if tariffs remain in place or increase. As highlighted in the previous research piece by Morgan Stanley, retailers could see as much as a 35% hit to earnings per share.

Highlighted in JPMorgan's global research piece, investors and businesses are unsure of what to expect next in the tariff fight. The uncertainty makes it difficult for businesses to impact sales, pricing, and consumer preferences. From an investor's standpoint, the uncertainty makes it difficult to gauge future cash flows and revenue growth. Despite the looming threat, Shoe Carnival's strong balance sheet and high free cash flow generation position it well to handle the uncertain macroeconomic period.

Conclusion

As a small-cap value stock in the retail sector, Shoe Carnival has become an out of favor investment. The company has lost more than 50% of its value in the last year, but investors who are willing to take a contrarian approach should be rewarded well.

For the first time in its history, the company now has a dividend yield over 3% - all while growing the dividend at a compound annual growth rate of 28% over the last five years. Shoe Carnival's conservative balance sheet, low payout ratio and ability to generate substantial free cash flow provide investors with the security they need to know their dividends are well funded and will continue to grow.

data.title: GFLW: 2025 Gains, AUM Growth, Factor Mix Make This ETF A Buy (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Continuing coverage of the VictoryShares Free Cash Flow Growth ETF (NASDAQ:GFLW), a passively managed fund with a strategy at the intersection of quality and growth, today I would like to upgrade this vehicle to a Buy.

To recap, in January of this year, while emphasizing its numerous advantages like the 25.3% weighted average 3-year free cash flow CAGR of its portfolio, I opted for a Hold rating, as GFLW had just a few weeks in the books back then, and placing a lot of hope in a debutant, even assuming a seemingly capable strategy, is precarious. Moreover, its performance had not been particularly strong.

However, since then, a few cogent reasons for an upgrade have emerged.

  • GFLW has seen a massive growth in assets under management that have risen 3.5x since January and recently surpassed a $500 million milestone, indicating robust investor interest.
  • This was in part facilitated by its decent performance this year, as it has not only beaten the S&P 500 index since my January article amid the tariff drama and other headwinds but also has delivered one of the best results in the Large Growth sub-class of the U.S. Equity class.
  • Its factor mix remains close to excellent when it comes to quality (including free cash flow) and growth exposures, plus it has a few advantages over the iShares Russell 1000 Growth ETF (IWF), which, I believe, could secure more upside in the current market environment.

So below, I would like to address these reasons in due detail as well as offer a few remarks on risks.

GFLW Strategy

But before we proceed to the discussion of the reasons for optimism, let us recap GFLW's strategy.

As explained in the prospectus available on its website, GFLW tracks the Victory Free Cash Flow Growth Index, which

... is designed to deliver exposure to equity securities of large-capitalization U.S. issuers of growth companies. To be included in the Index, these U.S. large-capitalization growth companies generally will have a positive “free cash flow growth trend” and have a high “free cash flow return on invested capital.”

Constituents are selected from the VettaFi US Equity Large/Mid-Cap 1000 Index. Neither "financial companies" nor REITs can join the index, which is understandable as the concept of FCF has limitations when applied to these industries.

The weighting method is quite complex:

The rules-based methodology assigns weightings by measuring a combination of total free cash flow and “absolute momentum,” which is defined as the absolute value of a company’s trailing 12-month, risk-adjusted total return and accounts for the magnitude of price fluctuations over the specified period without regard to whether the return is positive or negative.

There are a few constraints, which, I believe, are necessary to disperse risks more evenly. More specifically:

Individual companies are capped at 4% of the Index. The rules-based methodology also limits the amount of companies in any one sector. That is, companies in any one sector will not exceed 45% of the Index. In addition, the allocation of companies in any single sector (or “weighting”) will not exceed a weighting of more than 20% greater than the weighting of that sector in the Parent Index.

GFLW Performance

Despite the exceedingly challenging environment, GFLW has been delivering excellent results.

Since my January article, GFLW has solidly outperformed the S&P 500 index.

But why has the ETF been performing so strongly? There have been a few contributors. Comparing the holdings datasets as of January 28 and June 4, I have found out that they have an 87.8% overlap, as 82 stocks have retained their place in the portfolio. In this group, 45 stocks have delivered positive price returns, while 14 have seen gains of more than 20%. And the five main contributors were as follows:

Stock Weight (as of June 4) Price return
Palantir (PLTR) 4.44% 50.03%
Duolingo (DUOL) 0.73% 48.34%
ZScaler (ZS) 0.41% 46.52%
GE Vernova (GEV) 3.65% 35.58%
Spotify Technology (SPOT) 3.93% 34.13%

Calculated by the author using data from Seeking Alpha and the ETF. Based on the share prices as of January 28 and June 5

Next, since its inception in December 2024, GFLW has outpaced IWF, which tracks the index the fund compares its returns to, as well as the Pacer US Cash Cows Growth ETF (BUL), which has certain similarities with it strategy-wise. It has also outmaneuvered the U.S. market represented by the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV).

Moreover, in the Large Growth sub-class (the U.S. Equity class, 117 ETFs in total as of June 5), GFLW is the seventh top performer YTD, with a 7.4% price return. For a fuller context, my calculations show that its result is almost 3x higher than the median return in this sub-class, 2.51%.

GFLW Has Remarkable AUM Growth

One of the issues ETF investors should always keep an eye on is whether a vehicle they are interested in has a healthy trend in asset flows. The reason for that is simple: if AUM stagnates at a low level, the risk of a fund's liquidation increases.

In the case of GFLW, I can conclude that, at least for now, investor interest has been nothing short of robust (probably because its expense ratio of 39 bps is not too high for such a sophisticated strategy), as its AUM has recently surpassed a $500 million milestone, which is an excellent result for an ETF that has just six months in the books.

GFLW Has Convincing Factor Mix

As of June 4, there were 102 holdings in the GFLW portfolio, including cash & cash equivalents and future. 100 of them are labeled as 'COMMON STOCK' or 'COMMON STOCK LIKE' in the 'Security Type' column. As I said above, 82 of them could be found in the January version. AppLovin Corporation (APP) is the key holding in this mix, with a 4.7% weight.

Regarding sectors, investors should understand that GFLW is much more selective here than IWF and IVV, as it ignores five out of 11 GICS sectors, namely energy, financials, materials, real estate, and utilities.

I would argue that the factor mix of this portfolio is supportive of a bullish view. Why? Because its growth characteristics are much stronger than those of IVV, and there is close to nothing to criticize about its quality. Also, we see a few advantages over IWF. Let us look at the data:

Size and value factors

GFLW's is anything but inexpensive, as its metrics like the adjusted weighted average earnings yield, EV/EBITDA (adjusted for the impact of outliers with four- and triple-digit figures), and Price/Sales illustrate quite clearly. Only the free cash flow yield (which is also an indirect quality indicator) speaks in its favor.

Nevertheless, what, I believe, makes it appealing here is that its exposure to the trillion-dollar companies is smaller than those of IWF and IVV (11.6% weight vs. 56% in IWF and 35.8% in IVV), and this is reflected in its weighted average market cap. And the corollary here is that it certainly should appeal to investors who are on the lookout for growth, but in a better diversified portfolio that is not overexposed to the Magnificent Seven.

Growth

GFLW's main advantages here are

  • a spectacular 3-year free cash flow compound annual growth rate of its portfolio that dwarfs those of IWF and IVV,
  • the fact that it has the smallest exposure to companies that have seen their FCF shrank over the 3-year period,
  • its allocation to companies with a B- Quant Growth grade or higher, which is the largest in this ETF trio,
  • its weighted average forward revenue, EPS, and EBITDA growth rates that exceed those of IVV.

Also, its WA forward EPS growth rate is higher than IWF's.

As to the fact that IWF has stronger forward revenue and EBITDA growth rates, I attribute it to the impact of NVIDIA (NVDA), which has a 10.9% weight in IWF but only 2% in GFLW. I have already explained this phenomenon in the previous article, so today I would like to once again make an adjustment to prove that. More specifically, if we reduce NVDA's forward revenue growth rate to 20% from 60.3%, IWF's WA rate would drop to 12.8%, while GFLW's would be surprisingly higher at 13.8%.

Quality

For quality-focused investors, GFLW offers quite a lot.

  • For example, almost all its holdings have a B- Quant Profitability rating or higher. Both IWF and IVV have smaller allocations, though marginally.
  • Both GFLW's Return on Assets and adjusted Return on Equity are much higher than those of IVV. At the same time, IWF is ahead, but mostly because of NVDA.
  • And what impressed me most is its exposure to stocks that might be called analysts' darlings, as they have a B- EPS Revisions grade or stronger. Both IVV and IWF are well behind here.

Risks

This opportunity comes with risks, chief among them being GFLW's large growth factor exposure. On the one hand, this is an advantage the ETF can be justly proud of. On the other hand, a new round of the tariff drama, if it emerges, and its impact on the perception of inflation dynamics, monetary policy, and possibility of a recession can easily result in a new wave of growth premia adjustments across the market (in other words, P/Es would start to shrink), so it is highly likely that high achievers that dominate GFLW's portfolio would see their prices sinking. This process could be exacerbated by the fact that the ETF has almost no defensive exposure, as it has no footprint in utilities and just 66 bps allocated to consumer staples. Besides, it is not to be forgotten that GFLW is a high-beta vehicle (i.e., the weighted average 24-month beta of its portfolio is at 1.38, as per my calculations).

Final Thoughts

Considering GFLW's convincing performance this year, its impressive AUM growth, and the nicely calibrated factor mix of its portfolio, I believe it is no longer necessary to defend the neutral view, so I upgrade this ETF to a Buy.

data.title: Worried About Tariff Passthrough Onto Prices? So Are Business Execs
data.description:
By Kevin Foster, Aaron Jalca and Brent Meyer

Over the past couple of months, newswires have focused on the potential for elevated tariff rates to feed through into higher inflation and potentially affect output growth as well. Indeed, Chair Powell, in his last post-FOMC meeting press conference said, "What looks likely, given the scope and scale of the tariffs, is that… the risks to higher inflation, higher unemployment have increased."

Recent research from economists at the Atlanta Fed suggests that if firms are able to pass through all the costs of tariffs, retail prices would increase significantly - as much as 1.6 percent (depending on how effective tariff rates evolve from here).

And even at a 50 percent passthrough rate, the impact on prices would be large enough to be felt in the aggregate (0.8 percent increase in retail prices). How plausible is full passthrough? Going back to the last episode with rising tariffs in 2018, research showed that the cost of the tariffs was almost entirely passed through onto domestic prices.

Rising tariff rates pose threats to the real side of the economy as well. Another highly cited academic paper on tariffs points out that in a simple supply and demand model, "higher prices also reduce demand by domestic consumers."

In this environment, where policy changes lead to sharp increases in costs for many firms, we were curious about how firms would respond, especially in light of a potential reduction in demand that typically accompanies a price hike.

So, we turned to the Atlanta Fed's Business Inflation Expectations survey (BIE), a monthly survey of Sixth District firms that is well positioned to ask timely questions on economic conditions facing firms. In gathering information for the April 2025 BIE survey, we asked firms about their ability to pass through increased costs caused by a new economic policy without a resulting reduction in demand.

To that end, we posed two questions to firms about their ability to pass through a hypothetical cost increase to consumers. One of the questions was, "Suppose a new economic policy causes unit costs for your main good or service to increase by [10 or 25] percent, effective immediately. Based on current levels of demand for your main good or service, how much of that cost increase would your firm be able to pass through to customers?"

The survey was in fielded from April 7 through April 18, at a time when 10 percent was the baseline tariff for almost all countries, and 25 percent was the tariff on all foreign-made autos and auto parts. Some countries or regions, including China and the European Union, had much higher tariffs at the time.

The interesting twist in this line of questioning is the inclusion of the phrase "Based on current levels of demand." The interpretation here is that firms are telling us how much of the cost increase they would be able to pass through to customers before it had a negative impact on demand for that good or service.

Source: Atlanta Fed's Business Inflation Expectations survey • Download data
